content:
Review the following design for an asynchronous file-backed cache. Identify the most important correctness and performance risks, then propose a safer design. Keep the review concrete, prioritize issues rather than listing every possible improvement, and stay under 500 words.

The service stores immutable objects in files. Each request supplies an object key. A dictionary maps keys to slots, and every slot owns a reusable byte buffer. On a hit, the service returns a view into the slot buffer. On a miss, it chooses the slot with the oldest timestamp, updates the dictionary immediately, and starts an asynchronous read into that slot. Reads run on a global concurrent executor. The caller awaits the read future and then uses the returned view.

To reduce lock contention, the dictionary is protected only while a key is looked up or inserted. Slot timestamps, in-flight state, and buffer contents are not protected. Two misses may therefore choose the same oldest slot. The implementation assumes this is harmless because both callers await their own reads. A hit is allowed while a replacement read is in flight, because the new dictionary entry already points to the selected slot. Failed reads leave the new key in the dictionary so a later request can retry using the same slot.

The file format begins with a fixed header containing payload length and checksum. The read task reads the header, resizes the reusable buffer if necessary, then reads the payload. It verifies the checksum after publishing completion. There is no upper bound on payload length beyond the integer type. Cancellation stops waiting in the caller but does not cancel the executor task. Metrics count a cache hit whenever the dictionary lookup succeeds, including in-flight and failed entries.

The service is expected to handle many concurrent requests, repeated keys, occasional I/O failures, and memory pressure. Objects are immutable after they are written, but files can be replaced atomically by another process. Returned views may be retained briefly after the request finishes. The design goal is bounded memory, no corrupted reads, deduplicated work for the same key, and useful hit-rate and latency metrics.
